Swapon: Media commission, policy to be formed by July-August
item.description:
Information and Broadcasting Minister Zahir Uddin Swapon on Tuesday said a time-befitting media policy and a media commission will be formed by July-August next to ensure the interests of all parties - journalists, owners and newspaper employees.
Besides, he said, the media commission will work to bring the issues of creating anarchy through unethical journalism under the legal framework.
The information and broadcasting minister made the remarks at a weekly press briefing held at the conference room of the Press Information Department (PID) at the Secretariat here on Tuesday morning.
Prime Minister's Information and Broadcasting Adviser Dr Zahed Ur Rahman, Additional Secretary of the ministry Md Yasin and Principal Information Officer Syed Abdal Ahmed were present.
About the wage board for journalists and media management, Swapon said both protecting the interests of journalists and patronising the owners of the media institutions are the responsibility of the government.
“We are working to create a specific and organised legal framework to resolve disputes between journalists and the owners. A policy and a commission acceptable to all will be formed by July-August so that the interests of all parties are guaranteed,” he said.
The minister said the issues of false cases filed against journalists and unethical journalism were discussed in the meeting with the Editors' Council.
Since there is no specific provision in the existing law to identify such activities as a crime, aggrieved people are often using different laws, he said. Swapon said it will be possible to remove this legal limitation after the formation of the proposed media commission.
Referring to the decision of the Cabinet, he said the government has taken a policy decision to give maximum encouragement to the import of environment-friendly electric buses in the country.
In particular, arrangements are being made so that educational institutions can bring these buses with duty-free facilities, he said.
“If a bus is introduced in an educational institution, the guardians of 50 students will not have to use their separate cars, which will ease traffic congestion and pollution,” he said.
The minister said the implementation work of creating a specific definition and policy on how many seat-capacity buses can be brought duty-free for public transport has already begun.

item.title: Speaker: State responsible to eliminate inequalities at all levels of society
item.description:
Speaker of the National Parliament Building Hafiz Uddin Ahmad, Bir Bikram, on Tuesday said it is the responsibility of the state to eliminate inequalities at every level of society.
He said the All-Party Caucus can work in addressing inequalities at different levels of society and the country, and thus would help ensure formal recognition of the contributions of marginalized and disadvantaged communities to the country’s development.
The speaker made the remarks when a delegation led by Dr Debapriya Bhattacharya, Convener of the Citizen’s Platform for SDGs, Bangladesh, and also Distinguished Fellow of the Centre for Policy Dialogue (CPD), paid a courtesy call on him at the latter’s office at the Jatiya Sangsad Bhaban.
During the meeting, they exchanged views on the role of the caucus in advancing disadvantaged, underprivileged and marginalized communities through the implementation of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly the SDG goals relating to peace, justice and strong institutions.
They also discussed the role of the Caucus to ensure the country’s overall development as well as access to education, healthcare and safe food for disadvantaged communities through the implementation of the government’s political commitments with institutional and technical assistance, transparency and accountability.
The delegation of Citizen’s Platform, Bangladesh, congratulated Hafiz Uddin Ahmad on being elected to the post of the speaker and also expressed condolences over the recent death of his wife, noted educationist Dilara Hafiz.

item.title: BB buys $85m from 6 banks in a day
item.description:
Bangladesh Bank has once again bought dollars from the market to maintain stability in the country's foreign exchange market.
On Tuesday (May 19, 2026), the central bank bought a total of $85 million from six commercial banks. The cut-off rate per dollar has been set at Tk122.75.
Sources in Bangladesh Bank said that the central bank's total dollar purchases so far in May have stood at $395 million. Since the beginning of FY26, the total dollar purchases have increased to $606 million.
Analysts say that in recent months, there has been a positive change in the supply of foreign exchange due to increased expatriate income and export earnings in the country.
As a result, additional dollars are being deposited in the hands of banks. The central bank is regularly buying dollars from the market to prevent excessive depreciation of the dollar in the market and to strengthen reserves.
According to economists, the economy, which has been under pressure from the dollar crisis, rising import costs and dwindling reserves for several years, is now gradually easing.
The supply of dollars in the interbank market has increased, especially due to the increase in remittance flows through legal channels, improvement in export earnings and some control over import costs.
In this situation, the central bank's dollar purchase is being seen as a strategy to create a balance in the market.
Bangladesh Bank officials say that when the supply of dollars is high in the market, the central bank buys dollars.
When a crisis occurs, it sells dollars in the market. Through this, an attempt is made to keep the exchange rate relatively stable.
Those concerned believe that the central bank's large dollar purchase in the current fiscal year will play an important role in rebuilding the country's foreign exchange reserves. At the same time, it will also increase confidence in the country's foreign trade and exchange markets.
Bankers say that there is currently no intense pressure on the dollar in the market as before.
Many banks now have more dollars than needed. As a result, the central bank is getting the opportunity to withdraw dollars from the market.
On one hand, this increases reserves, and on the other hand, the risk of a sudden drop in the dollar rate is also decreasing.
According to economic analysts, the central bank's continued dollar purchases indicate an improvement in the country's foreign exchange situation.
However, to maintain this positive trend, they believe it is important to increase export earnings, continue remittance flows, and maintain a balance in import management.

item.title: Budget FY27: Steel traders urge not to raise electricity prices
item.description:
Steel sector traders in the country demanded not to increase electricity prices again at a press briefing on Tuesday.
According to them, the steel industry is currently in a critical state due to the increase in gas and electricity prices, the rise in the dollar price, high interest rates and stagnation in the construction sector in the last few years. In this situation, if electricity prices are increased again, many factories will be forced to reduce or close production.
Organized by the Bangladesh Steel Manufacturers Association (BSMA), its president Mohammad Jahangir Alam said that the long-term impact of the Covid-19 pandemic, increase in global energy and raw material prices after the Russia-Ukraine War, the dollar crisis, exchange rate instability and the ongoing conflict in the Middle East have put pressure on the country's industrial sector.
At the same time, the demand for steel has also decreased significantly due to the decrease in government and private construction work.
He said that currently, high bank interest rates, limited credit facilities, gas crisis, increasing import costs and a shortage of working capital are creating additional pressure on the industrial sector.
In the meantime, if the price of electricity is increased again, the production cost will increase further and the industrial sector of Bangladesh will lag behind in the international market.
Alam claimed that in the last few years, the price of electricity in the industrial sector has increased by about 30%. On the other hand, in some cases, the price of gas has increased by about 300%.
He also said that large factories in the steel sector receive electricity directly from the national grid. As a result, there is no scope for system loss, transmission loss or distribution loss in this sector. Still, various unreasonable charges like demand charges, additional VAT and power factor-related fines are being imposed on industrial establishments, which are actually increasing the price of electricity further.
The organization demanded that the price of electricity for the steel industry not only be increased, but also that unreasonable demand charges and additional VAT be reduced.
At the same time, a call was made to re-evaluate the policy of additional charges based on power factor. The leaders said that in order to ensure industrialization and investment-friendly energy policy, it is necessary to ensure uninterrupted and quality power supply for industrial establishments.
It was informed at the press conference that currently there are 35 factories in the steel sector in the country.
The combined annual production capacity of these factories is about 12 million tonnes. While the demand for steel in the country is 70 to 75 million tonnes in normal times, it has now come down to 40 to 45 million tonnes.
As a result, the factories are now using less than 40% of their production capacity.
BSMA secretary general Sumon Chowdhury said that a public hearing will be held the day after tomorrow regarding the increase in electricity prices. There will be a demand for setting a separate electricity price for the steel industry.

item.title: Berger Training Institute, NHRDF launch free govt training for decorative painters
item.description:
A Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) was signed between the National Human Resources Development Fund (NHRDF) and Berger Training Institute (BTI) on May 10, 2026, to provide free training for decorative painters.
On behalf of the National Human Resources Development Fund, Md Khairuzzaman Majumder, chairman (NHRDF), secretary, Finance Division, Ministry of Finance, and on behalf of Berger Training Institute, Md Salahuddin Ahmed, chairman of BTI and chief marketing officer of Berger Paints, along with concerned officials were present.
He said: “I sincerely congratulate the government for successfully completing 7 batches of the Painting for Construction Level-2 Short Course as per the first agreement with the government and for the second time renewing the Level-2 Short Course to work together to create skilled manpower in Dhaka as well as Rajshahi.”
He also hoped that in the future, the country will contribute to the development of the country through a wider range of training.
Berger Training Institute, the only registered institution in the paint industry by the National Skill Development Authority (NSDA), has trained 15,000 painters and issued skill certificates since its inception in 2016.

item.title: MTB Foundation hosts flag-off ceremony for Himalayan expedition
item.description:
MTB Foundation, as part of its project titled ‘Climbing for Climate, Building for Communities - A Climate Resilience Initiative of MTB Foundation’ recently organized a flag-off ceremony for a pioneering Bangladeshi mountaineer, Yasmin Lisa, as she embarks on her high-altitude Himalayan expedition.
The flag-off ceremony marked the naming of Yasmin Lisa as a climate change ambassador of MTB Foundation in its mission to implement impact-driven initiatives aimed at strengthening climate resilience at the grassroots level.
Inspired by Begum Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain’s feminist utopian story ‘Sultana's Dream’, MTB Foundation, as part of its commitment to strengthening women empowerment, supports Sultana’s Dream Unbound of Abhijatri, founded by Nishat Mazumder, Bangladesh's first female Mount Everest conqueror.
The initiative is expected to reinforce MTB Foundation’s efforts to encourage women from all levels to break barriers and lead with dignity, thus, ensuring inclusive development.
The flag-off ceremony was attended by Syed Mahbubur Rahman, managing director & CEO, Rais Uddin Ahmad, deputy managing director & CAMLCO from Mutual Trust Bank PLC (MTB), and Samia Chowdhury, chief executive officer from MTB Foundation. Nishat Mazumder, co-founder of Abhijatri, Yasmin Lisa, renowned mountaineer and other officials from Abhijatri also attended the event.

item.title: Tecno celebrates Eid-ul-Azha mega gift winners at flagship store
item.description:
Tecno Bangladesh is making this Eid-ul-Azha more exciting for customers through its ongoing Eid-ul-Azha mega campaign, where lucky customers are winning exciting mega gifts and cashback rewards.
As part of the campaign, Tecno hosted a special Mega Gifts Giveaway Event on recently at the Tecno Flagship Store, Centrepoint, Uttara, Dhaka.
The event was attended by Tecno officials along with lucky winners of the campaign, where prizes were officially handed over to customers during the special giveaway ceremony.
After purchasing Tecno Camon 50 Ultra, Md Jamal Uddin from Basabo, Dhaka became the lucky winner of an electric bike, while Nasim from Joypurhat received a laptop through the campaign.
Along with them, many customers who chose the Pova Curve 2 and Camon 50 Ultra also enjoyed special rewards from Tecno, including Tecno Buds, backpacks, and other AIoT devices.
The lucky winners celebrated their memorable Eid moments with Tecno at the flagship store.
The campaign officially started on May 1, 2026 and will continue until Eid-ul-Azha, giving more customers the opportunity to win exciting gifts and cashback rewards.
Through this campaign, customers who purchase any smartphone from the Tecno Camon Series or Pova Series can get the chance to win exciting rewards, including an electric bike, laptop, Tecno AIoT products, and cashback of up to Tk10,000.
The campaign also includes gifts such as TWS earbuds, smartwatches, and other exclusive rewards for lucky customers.
Tecno encourages customers to visit their nearest outlets and purchase eligible Camon or Pova Series smartphones for a chance to become the next lucky winner this Eid-ul-Azha.
For more information, visit Tecno Bangladesh’s official website or follow Tecno on its official social media platforms.

item.title: State seeks stay on High Court order barring Ivy’s arrest without specific case
item.description:
The state has filed a petition seeking a stay on a High Court order that barred authorities from showing former mayor of Narayanganj City Corporation Selina Hayat Ivy arrested or harassing her without a specific case.
Ivy’s lawyer, Advocate Md Motahar Hossain Saju, confirmed the matter to Bangla Tribune on Tuesday.
Ivy was arrested on May 9, 2025, around 3am from Chunka Kutir in the Deobhog area of Narayanganj . She was later shown arrested in multiple cases.
The High Court granted her bail in five cases, but the Appellate Division later stayed the bail orders following appeals by the state.
On November 18, she was shown arrested in five more cases. She secured bail in those cases on February 26, though the Appellate Division again stayed the bail after state appeals.
Later, the Appellate Division upheld the bail granted in all 10 cases.
Meanwhile, police sought permission to show Ivy arrested in two murder cases filed with Siddhirganj Police Station on September 11, 2024, and June 30, 2025.
The Narayanganj chief judicial magistrate approved the applications on March 2 in one case and April 12 in the other.
A writ petition was subsequently filed challenging the legality of showing her arrested in the cases.
Following a hearing, the High Court on April 26 issued a rule asking why the arrests in the two Siddhirganj cases should not be declared illegal and contrary to fundamental rights.
The court also ordered authorities not to show Ivy arrested or harass her except in connection with specific cases.
The state later moved the Appellate Division seeking a stay on that order.

item.title: Jamaat secretary general visits ailing Mirza Abbas in Malaysia
item.description:
Jamaat-e-Islami Secretary General Golam Parwar visited BNP Standing Committee member Mirza Abbas, MP, pat Prince Court Hospital in Malaysia, where he is currently undergoing treatment.
During the visit, Parwar met doctors and was briefed on Mirza Abbas’s health condition.
He prayed for his speedy recovery.
Barrister Najibur Rahman Momen was also present.

item.title: Trump says holding off on new Iran attack
The silent trauma of children witnessing domestic violence
Bangladesh to celebrate Eid-ul-Azha on May 28
NOAB announces five-day Eid-ul-Azha holiday
Speakers: Bangladesh risks backward slide on judicial independence
Security guard dies after falling from rooftop in Khilgaon
item.description:
A security guard died after falling from the rooftop of a building in the Sipahibagh area of Khilgaon in the capital on Monday night.
The deceased was identified as Habibur Rahman, 45, son of Matiur Rahman from Ahmadpur village under Gauripur upazila in Mymensingh.
According to building owner Abu Taher, the incident took place around 10:45pm at house no. 671/6/A in Sipahibagh, Khilgaon, when Habibur went to the rooftop of the six-storey building and fell to the ground.
Confirming the matter, Sub-Inspector Ershad of Khilgaon Police Station said CCTV footage showed the victim climbing onto the railing while talking on his mobile phone before falling.
He was first taken to a local hospital and later rushed to the emergency department of Dhaka Medical College Hospital, where doctors declared him dead at around 11:30pm after examination.
Police said Habibur worked as a security guard at the building. His body has been kept at the Dhaka Medical College Hospital morgue for an autopsy. Authorities said the incident is under investigation.

item.title: Apologetic farewell: A ride on Griezmann’s redemptive Atletico run
Mushfiq majesty: Landmark ton leaves Pakistan chasing daunting 437
Experts: Urgent reforms can restore economic stability, drive job creation
IFIC Bank signs agreement with US-Bangla Airlines

German Business Council hosts networking dinner at Westin Dhaka

UN demands Israel prevent ‘genocide’ in Gaza
Dhaka air 5th worst in world Tuesday morning
item.description:
Bangladesh’s capital Dhaka, ranked fifth among the world’s most polluted cities on Tuesday morning, recording an Air Quality Index (AQI) score of 148 at 9:45am.
According to the latest AQI data, Pakistan’s Lahore topped the list with a hazardous score of 367, followed by India’s Delhi in second position with a score of 204.
Kampala secured the third spot with an AQI reading of 164, indicating unhealthy air quality levels.
Dhaka’s AQI score of 148 placed it in the “unhealthy for sensitive groups” category, meaning people with respiratory issues, children and the elderly may experience health effects.
The AQI is an index used to report daily air quality, indicating how clean or polluted the air is and what associated health effects might be a concern.
Air pollution in Dhaka has remained a persistent problem, particularly during the dry season, due to factors such as construction work, brick kilns, vehicle emissions and dust.
Health experts advise residents to limit prolonged outdoor activities and use protective measures, such as masks, during periods of poor air quality.

DMP steps up Eid-ul-Azha security, vows crackdown on crime
item.description:
Newly appointed Dhaka Metropolitan Police (DMP) Commissioner Mosleh Uddin Ahmed on Monday said special police drives against extortionists, muggers and drug traders are continuing ahead of Eid-ul-Azha 2026, urging all to play responsible roles in keeping the city and the state safe and functional.
He made the remarks while presiding over a coordination meeting on overall security and traffic management in the city for the upcoming Eid-ul-Azha at the conference room of the DMP headquarters.
“The state and the city belong to all of us. Everyone has to play a responsible role from their respective positions to keep the city and the state beautiful and functional,” the DMP commissioner said.
He also said launches would not be allowed to carry excess passengers during the Eid period and urged the authorities concerned not to carry out road excavation works in the city at this time.
The DMP commissioner called on all government agencies to work in coordination to ensure safe and hassle-free Eid travel for people.
Representatives from different intelligence agencies, specialised police units, the Department of Shipping, Bangladesh Road Transport Owners’ Association, Bangladesh Road Transport Federation, Bangladesh Truck-Covered Van Owners’ Association, Bangladesh Truck Drivers Workers Federation, Bangladesh Bus-Truck Owners’ Association, launch workers’ association, BKMEA, BGMEA, Gazipur Metropolitan Police (GMP), BIWTA, Roads and Highways Department, Dhaka South City Corporation, Dhaka North City Corporation, Fire Service and Civil Defence, bus and launch owners’ associations, Bangladesh Inland Water Transport organisation and several government and private service-providing agencies attended the meeting.
At the beginning of the meeting, DMP Joint Commissioner (Operations) Mohammad Shahidullah presented the overall security plan through a PowerPoint presentation.
Representatives of different organisations later took part in an open discussion and shared their opinions.
Among others, DMP Additional Commissioner (Crime and Operations) SN Md Nazrul Islam, Additional Commissioner (Counter Terrorism and Transnational Crime) Md Masud Karim, Additional Commissioner (DB) Md Shafiqul Islam, Additional Commissioner (Traffic) Md Anisur Rahman, joint commissioners, deputy commissioners and police officials of different ranks were present.

item.title: Neymar says he is fully fit and ready for World Cup despite criticism
AI policing grows, but battery rickshaws remain untamed
Powering our future
Court orders seizure of tax files of three ex-ministers’ wives
item.description:
A Dhaka court on Monday ordered the seizure of income tax records of Nadira Mahmud, widow of former industries minister Nurul Majid Mahmud Humayun, and Arifa Jesmin Konika, wife of former state minister for ICT Zunaid Ahmed Palak.
Dhaka Metropolitan Special Judge Md Sabbir Faiz issued the order, granting two separate petitions filed by the Anti-Corruption Commission (ACC).
According to the case filed by the ACC on April 8, 2025, Nadira Mahmud is accused of acquiring assets worth Tk 1.34 crore beyond known sources of income.
The commission alleged that the assets were obtained through illegal means for personal financial gain. For investigative purposes, the court permitted the seizure of her complete income tax records from the beginning of her tax history up to the 2024–25 tax year, including all permanent and miscellaneous files.
In a separate case filed on December 12, 2024, Arifa Jesmin Konika and her husband, former state minister Zunaid Ahmed Palak, are accused of jointly abusing power to accumulate illegal assets worth Tk 9.58 crore.
They are also under investigation over suspicious financial transactions involving deposits of Tk 22.34 crore and withdrawals of Tk 17.56 crore through 31 bank accounts, which the ACC has described as potential money laundering.
The ACC told the court that access to Konika’s income tax records from the 2008–09 to 2024–25 tax years is necessary for the proper investigation of the allegations.

item.title: Prime Bank, Pandamart host special campaign on Mother’s Day
item.description:
To make Mother’s Day truly memorable, Prime Bank PLC and Pandamart, one of the country’s most popular online grocery platforms, recently organized a joint special campaign.
The initiative not only encouraged customers but also created a unique opportunity to honour mothers.
A special ceremony was held recently to celebrate the successful conclusion of the campaign and felicitate the winners.
Under the campaign, 10 customers who placed the highest-value orders on the Pandamart platform on 8 May were selected as winners.
Participating in this special competition, customers shopped spontaneously and secured their positions as top spenders.
Pandamart and Prime Bank designed the campaign specifically to honour the mothers of these ten customers.
In tribute to the winners’ mothers, a special dinner was arranged at ‘Tarka’, a popular restaurant in the capital.
The event, aimed at expressing love and gratitude to mothers, was attended by the winning customers and their family members. In a joyous and celebratory atmosphere, the dinner offered a heartfelt tribute to the mothers, creating an emotional ambience for all present.
The joint collaboration between Pandamart and Prime Bank is essentially an effort to make customer service more dynamic and vibrant.
Offering such recognition and opportunities to customers on special days plays a positive role in increasing people’s interest in digital transactions and online shopping.
The organizers have announced plans to arrange more innovative, human-centric campaigns for customers in the future. This initiative has highlighted not only the business relationship between the two organizations but also their sense of social responsibility.

item.title: Banks get weeklong holiday during Eid-ul-Azha
item.description:
The scheduled banks of the country will remain closed for seven days on the occasion of the upcoming Eid-ul-Azha.
Bangladesh Bank has announced that banking activities will be completely closed from May 25 (Monday) to May 31 (Sunday).
The central bank issued a circular on Sunday (May 18) in this regard, where it said that in continuation of the notification of the Ministry of Public Administration, branches and sub-branches of all scheduled banks of the country will be open as per normal schedule on May 23 (Saturday) and May 24 (Sunday).
However, special measures have been taken before Eid to pay salaries, bonuses and other allowances to workers and employees of the readymade garment industry and to keep import-export activities running.
For this, bank branches in Dhaka metropolis, Ashulia, Tongi, Gazipur, Savar, Bhaluka, Narayanganj and RMG-related areas of Chittagong will be open on a limited basis on May 25-26.
According to the Bangladesh Bank instructions, the office hours of bank branches on those two days will be from 10 am to 3 pm. Transactions will continue from 10 am to 1 pm.
In addition, import-export activities have been ordered to continue on a limited basis in bank branches, sub-branches and booths in sea, land and airport areas.
The concerned branches have been asked to continue essential banking activities during the Eid holidays (May 25 to May 31, excluding Eid day).
The instructions also state that officers and employees on duty on holidays will receive allowances as per government rules.

item.title: Jacob Elordi chooses risk over romance
‘Ulya’ brings Latvian basketball legend to Cannes
JU students suspend protest, give ultimatum over attempted rape incident
item.description:
Students of Jahangirnagar University on Sunday suspended their ongoing protest movement over the attempted rape of a female student after issuing a fresh three-day ultimatum to the university administration.
Earlier in the day, agitating students locked the university’s administrative building around 10:30am and enforced a blockade that continued until 7pm.
During the protest, several senior university officials, including the vice-chancellor and pro-vice-chancellors, were unable to enter their offices, causing disruption to a number of important administrative activities.
University Registrar ABM Azizur Rahman told Dhaka Tribune in the afternoon that protesters had locked the registrar's building with a set of demands while university officials and employees were still inside.
Later in the evening, Vice-Chancellor Professor Mohammad Kamrul Ahsan visited the protest site in front of the registrar's building.
“The students’ movement is justified. We could not properly fulfil our responsibilities,” the vice-chancellor said while addressing the demonstrators.
Speaking on behalf of the protesters, student representative Naziha Binte Shamsuddin demanded that the investigation report be presented before students during office hours on May 20 and that the accused be identified by the same day.
Responding to the demand, the vice-chancellor said it would not be possible to complete the investigation report within the next three days without affecting the quality of the inquiry.
“Still, I will discuss the matter with the members of the investigation committee,” he added.
Following the assurance, protesters removed the lock from the registrar building in the evening. However, they continued their lockout of the proctor’s office.
Meanwhile, students said three individuals had been detained in connection with separate incidents on campus. Around 10pm, they were handed over to police from the university’s security office.
Officials from Ashulia Police Station said one person was detained over allegations of harassing and verbally abusing a female student near the Islamnagar gate area.
Two others, including an employee of a campus shop, were detained for allegedly recording videos of protesting female students in front of the proctor’s office without consent.
Earlier, on May 13, students had given the administration a 48-hour ultimatum to arrest the accused in the attempted rape case. After the deadline passed without any arrest, they launched a series of demonstrations, including protest marches, sit-ins in front of the vice-chancellor’s office, declaring the proctor “unwanted,” and locking the proctor’s office.

item.title: BNP’s Ashim calls for the judiciary accountable to people, not governments
item.description:
Barrister Nasir Uddin Ahmed Ashim, international affairs secretary of the Bangladesh Nationalist Party, on Monday stressed the need for a judiciary that remains accountable to the people rather than any government or political force.
He made the comments at a seminar titled on the importance of a fully independent judiciary in Bangladesh, organized by the Dhaka Tribune and the Royal Norwegian Embassy in Dhaka.
Speaking at the event, Ashim said that despite constitutional commitments made more than five decades ago, Bangladesh still has “a long way to go” in ensuring full judicial independence.
Referring to Article 22 of the Constitution, which calls for the separation of the judiciary from the executive, he said the spirit of judicial independence must be protected constitutionally rather than relying on political goodwill.
Drawing comparisons with Norway, Ashim said even mature democracies took centuries to institutionalize safeguards for judges.
“It took Norway nearly two centuries to strengthen constitutional protections for judges. We must ask ourselves where we stand today,” he said.
Ashim argued that judicial independence is not only a matter of legal provisions but also of institutional courage and mentality.
Citing landmark rulings such as the Eighth Amendment case and the 16th Amendment verdict, he said Bangladesh’s higher judiciary had previously demonstrated independence by declaring unconstitutional actions unlawful, even during periods of authoritarian rule.
“When judges truly feel independent, they dare to declare unconstitutional acts invalid, regardless of who is in power,” he said.
“We want a judiciary that is accountable to the people of this country, not always to the government or any particular political party,” he said.
Ashim also called for greater transparency in judicial appointments and referred to models used abroad, including Norway’s judicial appointment structure involving judges, lawyers, civil servants, and public representatives.
He said the BNP is working on proposals related to judicial reform as part of its broader political agenda and remains open to criticism and recommendations from stakeholders.
Concluding his remarks, Ashim said judicial independence must serve the interests of justice, human rights and equality before the law.
Ashim said: “The government may change but the Judiciary must not change. The Constitution should remain the same. Any transition, any government comes to power, but it doesn't matter. We need to develop the system. That would be the ethos of this seminar.”
He also said the judiciary should remain open to public criticism and accountability, adding: “The judiciary is not above the law. We want such types of independence.”

item.title: President Shahabuddin returns home after medical check-up in London
item.description:
President Mohammed Shahabuddin has returned home after completion of his follow-up treatment and health examinations in London.
The president arrived in Dhaka on Monday morning, said his Press Secretary Sarwar Alam.
A significant block was detected in the heart of President Mohammed Shahabuddin during a routine medical check-up in the United Kingdom, prompting immediate angioplasty and placement of a stent.
The stent implantation procedure was performed at Royal Papworth Hospital in Cambridge on Tuesday afternoon.
Quoting president’s personal physician Colonel Dr AKM Faizul Haque, who is accompanying him in the UK, Sarwar told UNB that Shahabuddin underwent an angiogram under the supervision of interventional cardiologist Dr Stephen Holley at the hospital.
In October 2023, President Mohammed Shahabuddin underwent open-heart surgery at the National University Hospital (NUH) in Singapore.
Since then, he has been receiving follow-up treatment for heart-related complications at Royal Papworth Hospital in Cambridge.
Royal Papworth Hospital is internationally renowned for its advanced cardiac treatment and research and is recognised as one of the institutions involved in the world’s first successful heart transplant procedures.
The president left Dhaka for London on May 9 for health examinations.

item.title: Tangail without power for 6hrs after storm damages grid
item.description:
Power supply across Tangail remained disrupted for around six hours on Monday after a powerful storm damaged the 132kV transmission line, causing widespread suffering for residents.
According to officials of the Power Development Board (PDB), a heavy storm accompanied by lightning struck the district during the night. Two coils at the Bailla power grid station in Sadar upazila were reportedly damaged, forcing the shutdown of the entire 132kV line.
As a result, the whole district lost electricity from around 5am.
As of 11am, power supply had not yet been restored.
The prolonged outage disrupted emergency services, including hospital operations.
Although some institutions were managing operations through alternative arrangements, officials warned that an extended outage could severely affect healthcare and other essential services.
Residents said they were facing difficulties due to the inability to pump water using electric motors and feared food stored in refrigerators would spoil.
Khandaker Sadiqur Rahman, superintendent of Tangail General Hospital , said the hospital had been operating on generator power since morning.
“Medical services are being disrupted due to the outage,” he said.
Muhammad Anwarul Islam, executive engineer of the PDB, said repair work was underway.
“We hope to restore power supply soon,” he said.

item.title: Second namaz-e-janaza of Mizanur Rahman Sinha held in Naya Paltan
item.description:
The second namaz-e-janaza of former state minister for health, Mizanur Rahman Sinha, was held at Naya Paltan on Monday morning.
The janaza took place in front of the BNP central office, where party leaders and activists gathered to pay their respects.
BNP Senior Joint Secretary General Advocate Ruhul Kabir Rizvi, Joint Secretary General Habib-un-Nabi Khan Sohel, and other central leaders were present. Before the janaza, they reflected on Sinha’s political and personal life.
Rizvi said leaders like Mizanur Rahman Sinha should have lived longer, noting that he dedicated his life to the welfare of the country and its people.
He added that Sinha’s contributions to social development and the economy would be remembered.
Prayers were offered for the eternal peace of the departed soul.
His first janaza was held at the South Plaza of the National Parliament on Sunday, attended by BNP Chairman Tarique Rahman and Speaker Major (retd) Hafizuddin Ahmad, along with cabinet members.
Mizanur Rahman Sinha died on Friday night, while undergoing treatment at a hospital in Singapore. He was 83.
In his political career, he was elected to Parliament from Munshiganj-2 (Louhajang–Tongibari) as a BNP candidate in both the 1996 and 2001 elections.
After his 2001 victory, he served as state minister for Health and Family Welfare in the four-party alliance government led by former prime minister Khaleda Zia.
He also served for many years as BNP central treasurer and was later made convener of the Munshiganj District BNP committee.

item.title: Measles outbreak: 6 more children die in 24 hours, toll reaches 459
Kaarina Kaisar laid to rest in Munshiganj
Met Office forecasts rain in different parts of country
item.description:
Bangladesh Meteorological Department (BMD) on Monday forecasted rain in different parts of the country in next 24 hours commencing 9am.
"Light to moderate rain/thunder showers accompanied by temporary gusty/squally wind and lightning flashes is likely to occur at many places over Mymensingh and Sylhet divisions; at a few places over Rangpur, Dhaka and Chittagong divisions and at one or two places over Rajshahi, Khulna and Barisal divisions," said a Met office bulletin issued in the morning.
Besides, it said, moderately heavy to heavy falls may occur at places over Mymensingh and Sylhet divisions.
Day temperature may remain nearly unchanged, and night temperature may rise slightly over the country.
A mild heatwave is sweeping over the districts of Madaripur, Rajshahi, Pabna, Lakshmipur, Khulna, Satkhira and Jessore, and it may continue, the bulletin said.
The highest temperature on Sunday was recorded 36.6°C in Koyra under Khulna division, while Monday's minimum temperature is 20.5°C in Tangail.
The sun sets at 6:35pm on Monday and rises at 5:14am on Tuesday in Dhaka.

item.title: Aaron Rai storms to historic PGA Championship triumph
item.description:
Aaron Rai was just another name among a dozen others who had reason to believe the PGA Championship was theirs for the taking Sunday at tough Aronimink.
There was Rory McIlroy, Jon Rahm and Xander Schauffele, with 10 majors among them, all ready to seize the moment on a stage that was unfamiliar to Rai.
Justin Thomas was the clubhouse leader for nearly four hours after a 65 before the course turned hard under a hot sun.
What followed was a master class from golf's newest major champion.
Rai made six birdies over the last 10 holes, taking the lead for good on the 13th and pouring it on with a 70-foot birdie putt across the 17th green that evoked a roar so loud everyone else on the course must have realized it was over.
He closed with a 5-under 65 for a three-shot victory to become the first English-born player in more than a century to capture the PGA Championship.
“To be here is outside my wildest imagination,” Rai said.
Three shots behind as he approached the turn, Rai got back in the game with a 5-wood up the hill and a 40-foot eagle putt. His 40-yard bunker shot was sublime and set up a 6-foot birdie on the 13th hole, the reachable par 4 that moments earlier gobbled up McIlroy and Schauffele.
And then the 31-year-old Rai — who wore Ferrari shirts at junior golf tournaments because he once dreamed of being a Formula 1 driver — hit the gas with a back nine that ranks among the best in major championship history.
The previous two players to go 6 under or better over the final 10 holes of a major were Cameron Smith at St. Andrews when he won the 2022 British Open, and Jack Nicklaus when he won the 1986 Masters.
Rai now takes his place in some exclusive company.
Those chasing him with a better golfing pedigree — even Alex Smalley and Matti Schmid, who also took turns atop the leaderboard while going for their first win — were undone by untimely mistakes or failure to get good looks at birdie.
McIlroy, who closed with a 69, played the par 5s in even for the week and he chopped up the reachable par-4 13th for a bogey. He also glared and softly cursed at a fan who said “USA!” after McIlroy hit a wedge from the rough to the bunker on the par-5 16th. It was an indication McIlroy knew his hopes were all but gone.
Rai, who finished at 9-under 271, is the first player from England with his name on the Wanamaker Trophy since Jim Barnes in 1919, the second edition of this major and the first after World War I.
Rahm and Smalley tied for second, a big deal for both of them.
Rahm had his best finish in a major since defecting to LIV Golf at the end of 2023. He was slowed by a pair of bogeys on the front nine, and managed only one birdie on the back nine for a 68.
Smalley lost the lead with a messy double bogey on the sixth hole, and his best golf was too late. Rai already had his eye on the Wanamaker Trophy when Smalley made birdie on the 18th for a 70. The runner-up finish gets him in the next four majors, including the Masters.
Thomas made a 16-foot par putt on the final hole to post at 5-under 275, one shot behind as the final group was only in the second fairway. For the longest time, as Aronimink got tougher and the pressure got tighter, it looked like Thomas might have a chance.
Like everything else on this final day, Rai ended those hopes, too.
So concluded a most remarkable week in the Philadelphia suburbs when no one could separate themselves at Aronimink. The 22 players within four shots of the lead going into the final round was a PGA Championship record.
From that pack emerged the 31-year-old Rai, with one PGA Tour title, three on the European tour, and no finishes inside the top 15 at any of the majors.
He might not be well known among casual observers, but he is a star in the eyes of his peers for his humility and gracious personality.
“You won’t find one person on property who’s not happy for him,” McIlroy said.
“Super pumped for him and his team,” Schauffele said. “All-world gentleman, no doubt.”
He wears two gloves, a habit he started as a kid in England to battle the cold winters when he was practicing — and he was always practicing. Even more unusual for Rai is the plastic covers on each iron, a reminder of his roots.
He once said his father sacrificed to buy the nicest golf clubs and then would clean the grooves with baby oil after his son was done playing. Rai has left the iron covers on since then “to remember where I came from and to respect what I have.”
“Anybody that uses head covers in his irons because he coveted his irons when he was a kid so much that he wanted to respect the equipment and to still do it? Yeah, it shows a lot about a person,” Rahm said. “What he did today is nothing short of special.”
Rai had seven straight one-putt greens, the last one a 6-foot birdie putt on the 13th that made him the first player all week to reach 7 under. And then he kept right on going.
Thomas wound up in a for fourth with Ludvig Aberg (69) and Schmid, whose 5-foot par putt on the 18th hole gets him into his first Masters next year. Smith, who didn't drop a shot until the 17th hole, had a 68 to join McIlroy and Schauffele (69) another shot back.
Defending champion Scottie Scheffler missed a 4-foot birdie putt on the third hole and twice missed 3-foot par putts on the back nine in his closing round of 69 to tie for 14th, his first time out of the top 10 at a major since the 2024 U.S. Open.
Rai now has a five-year exemption on the PGA Tour, and into the Masters, U.S. Open and British Open. He can play the PGA Championship for life.
“Golf is an amazing game,” Rai said. “It teaches you so many things, and it teaches you so much humility and discipline and absolute hard work because nothing is ever given in this game.”
Nothing was given to him Sunday. Rai simply outplayed the strongest field in golf and won it.
Credit: AP/UNB

item.title: Messi shines as Inter Miami finally win at new stadium
item.description:
Lionel Messi's goal and dazzling assist finally earned Inter Miami a first win at their new stadium Sunday, beating the Portland Timbers 2-0.
The 38-year-old Argentine icon confounded the Timbers' defence all night long in a timely reminder of the danger he still poses at the World Cup, which kicks off in less than a month.
Messi struck first, starting and finishing a slick team goal for the defending Major League Soccer champions on the half-hour mark.
Messi laid off the ball to Luis Suarez, who found Telasco Segovia with a through ball. Segovia's instinctive backheel landed back at the feet of Messi, who swept it into the far corner from close range.
Ten minutes later, fellow Argentine Rodrigo De Paul romped up the right flank, finding Messi who played a one-two with Suarez.
The eight-time Ballon d'Or-winner then slalomed his way into the box, through four defenders, before using the outside of his left foot to tee up German Berterame for a tap-in.
Messi missed chances to add to his tally in a slower and goalless second half, curling a left-foot strike from outside the box just inches wide of the post.
And he nearly added a stunning free kick deep into stoppage time, denied by a superb James Pantemis save, who dove at full stretch and barely tipped the ball away from the top corner.
It was the fifth home game at the gleaming, purpose-built Nu Stadium, which was opened to great fanfare last month.
Until now, the 27,000-capacity arena had witnessed three frustrating draws and a humiliating Miami collapse from 3-0 up to lose 4-3 to Orlando City.
Despite their recent struggles at home, the win sends Inter Miami top of the Eastern Conference, one point clear of Nashville SC, who have two games in hand.
The World Cup, being held in the United States, Mexico and Canada, kicks off June 11.
Along with Cristiano Ronaldo, Messi -- who lifted the trophy with Argentina at the last edition, in Qatar -- is set to become the first player to appear in six different World Cups.

Dhaka air quality moderate Monday morning
item.description:
Dhaka, the densely populated capital of Bangladesh, ranked 12th among the world’s most polluted cities on Monday morning, recording an Air Quality Index (AQI) score of 83 at 8:40am.
According to the AQI index, Dhaka’s air quality was classified as ‘moderate’, posing a slight health risk.
Pakistan’s Lahore, India’s Delhi and Nepal’s Kathmandu topped the list of the world’s most polluted cities, with AQI scores of 188, 173 and 154 respectively.
An AQI between 101 and 150 is considered ‘unhealthy for sensitive groups’, while a score between 151 and 200 is classified as ‘unhealthy’. Readings between 201 and 300 are termed ‘very unhealthy’, and anything above 301 is regarded as ‘hazardous’, posing serious health risks.
The AQI measures daily air quality, indicating how clean or polluted the air is and the possible health effects on people.
In Bangladesh, the AQI is calculated based on five major pollutants — particulate matter (PM10 and PM2.5), nitrogen dioxide, carbon monoxide, sulphur dioxide and ozone.
Dhaka has long struggled with severe air pollution. Air quality usually worsens during winter and improves with the arrival of the monsoon season.
According to the World Health Organization (WHO), air pollution causes around seven million deaths globally each year, mainly from stroke, heart disease, chronic respiratory diseases, lung cancer and acute respiratory infections.

Call for setting up permanent rawhide market in Khulna
item.description:
A permanent rawhide market in Khulna could help traders avoid recurring losses during Eid-ul-Azha, local businessmen say.
Rawhide traders expressed frustration, alleging that the previous government failed to adopt business-friendly measures to protect the sector.
During a visit to the city’s Sheikhpara area on Sunday — the main hub for rawhide trading during Eid — traders demanded the establishment of a permanent market along with proper preservation facilities ahead of the festival season.
Every year, temporary rawhide markets are set up on roadsides during Eid-ul-Azha, with Sheikhpara emerging as the city’s primary trading centre. However, traders said no permanent market has yet been developed in Khulna.
According to them, no new rawhide trading hub has been established in the last seven years. The absence of storage and preservation facilities continues to create major difficulties during Eid-ul-Azha every year.
Babar Ali, a trader from Sheikhpara, said around 35 traders once operated from nearly 10 shops in the area. Over time, however, many shop owners became reluctant to rent out premises for rawhide businesses, forcing traders to gradually shut down operations.
He also said many traders incurred heavy losses after tannery companies failed to make timely payments.
“We need a permanent market near the existing trading zone. If proper facilities are ensured and hides are purchased at government-fixed prices, both traders and sellers will benefit,” he said.
Kartik Ghosh, general secretary of the Khulna Rawhide Traders Association, said traders are struggling due to the lack of preservation facilities.
“Sheikhpara’s temporary roadside rawhide market, locally known as Chamarpatti, was once a renowned trading centre. That tradition is now fading, and many traders have already left the business,” he said.
Association president Abdus Salam Dhali said the rawhide business in Khulna is on the verge of collapse.
He noted that government-fixed prices mainly apply to preserved and processed hides.
“We could not collect and preserve the expected volume of rawhides because of a shortage of labourers, inadequate tannery depots, and falling hide prices,” he said.
Khulna City Corporation (KCC) Administrator Nazrul Islam Manju said the city corporation would consider the issue if traders formally submit a proposal for a permanent rawhide market.
“We will consider where such a market can be established,” he added.
According to sources, of the total hides collected, 119,112 came from cows and buffaloes, while 207,682 were collected from goats, sheep, and other sacrificial animals.

item.title: Asaduzzaman: Law on enforced disappearance to be enacted to ensure strict punishment for offenders
item.description: Law, Justice and Parliamentary Affairs Minister Md Asaduzzaman said that a proper law will be enacted through consultations with stakeholders to ensure strict punishment for those involved in enforced disappearances. “There are many issues that need careful consideration. Such a law will not be enacted hastily if it ends up benefiting offenders instead of ensuring punishment,” he said. The minister made the remarks while presiding over a stakeholder meeting titled "Law on Enforced Disappearance" held in the evening at the CIRDAP auditorium in the capital, organized by the National Human Rights Commission. He further said: “We want to make a decision only after proper and extensive discussions on this matter. If a law is made hastily and it does not work in practice, and if offenders escape justice or benefit from it, then such a law has no value.” Md Asaduzzaman, a senior jurist himself, noted that enforced disappearance is a highly sensitive and serious issue, involving multiple considerations that will emerge through repeated discussions with stakeholders. The minister also said: “Be assured that a law will be enacted so that enforced disappearance can never return to Bangladesh.” “Speaking at the discussion, Sylhet-2 lawmaker Taslima Rushdie said, ‘On April 17, 2012, my husband, M Ilias Ali, was subjected to enforced disappearance with the direct involvement of the then state. We always believed he would return. We also hoped that after August 5, we might at least receive some information about him, but we did not.’” She said: “I entered politics seeking justice for victims of enforced disappearance and their families. I expect a law to be enacted so that no one in Bangladesh ever becomes a victim of enforced disappearance again.” Reserved seat Member of Parliament and coordinator of the human rights organization "Mayer Daak", Sanzida Islam Tuli, also attended the meeting. She said: “Previous human rights commissions have avoided this issue. We have never seen any effective steps from them. I believe that if the proposals from today’s discussion are implemented, it will help restore trust among the general public, victims of enforced disappearance, and their families.” Secretary of the Legislative and Parliamentary Affairs Division Dr Hafiz Ahmed Chowdhury delivered the welcome speech at the event. Others present included Attorney General Barrister Md Ruhul Quddus Kazal, UNDP Resident Representative Stefan Liller, lawyers, secretaries from various ministries, senior officials of the National Human Rights Commission, and representatives of various human rights organisations. During the open discussion, participants called for realistic legislation on enforced disappearance, including determining punishment based on the severity of the offence, setting minimum qualification standards for investigating officers, and introducing provisions to complete judicial proceedings within a fixed timeframe.

item.title: Speakers demand UN-backed river commission to resolve Ganges water crisis
item.description:
Speakers have demanded the formation of a regional river commission comprising China, Nepal, Bhutan, India, and Bangladesh under the supervision of the United Nations and the World Bank to ensure fair share of Ganges water.
They made the demand at a mass gathering organized by the ‘Farakka Long March Day Celebration Committee’ on the bank of the Padma River at Barokuthi in Rajshahi city on Saturday afternoon marking historic ‘Farakka Long March Day’.
The regional commission would be able to ensure equitable distribution of water in the river basins of the region, environmental conservation, economic development of the people of the river basins especially the poor, improving living standard of people and protecting the rights of neglected women and children, they observed.
Advocate Enamul Haque, President of the Rajshahi chapter of the ‘Save the River and Environment Movement’ presided over the function.
The speakers urged the government to take necessary steps to save the Sundarbans, the world’s largest mangrove forest and the UNESCO World Heritage, from the reduced river flow and excessive salinity.
They said that the damage caused to Bangladesh due to the diversion of water through India’s Farakka Barrage in the Ganges River, the dam on the Teesta River, and dams on other rivers must be assessed.
In this connection, they said that remedies and compensation must be demanded from the Indian government for causing harm to Bangladesh by unilaterally withdrawing water from the river Farakka.
However, this problem cannot be resolved bilaterally either. In the interest of a fair share of international rivers, a regional forum must be formed comprising India, Bangladesh, China, Nepal, and Bhutan on river issues, they observed.
Besides, the speakers said that if reservoirs are built in Nepal to store Himalayan water, the water flow at Farakka during the dry season could be increased from 130,000 cusecs to 190,000 cusecs.
Everyone would be benefitted from that. With the help of reservoirs, Nepal would be able to generate about 10,500 megawatts of electricity annually, which Nepal could also export to Bangladesh, they added.
Professor M. Rafiqul Islam, former Vice-Chancellor of Islamic University presented the key-note paper at the event while Prof. Nazrul Islam Mondol, Member of Parliament from Rajshahi-5 constituency, Mostafa Kamal Majumder, President of the International Farakka Committee, Ershad Ali Esha and Administrator of the District Council took part in the discussion.
Dr Wasim Hossain, President of DAB’s Rajshahi District Branch, river researcher Mahbub Siddiqui, Azad Khan Bhasani, Member Secretary of the Bhasani Council, and Sultan Mahmud Sumon, Vice President of the Rajshahi Interest Protection Committee also spoke on the occasion, among others.

item.title: Nor’wester causes widespread damage in Naogaon
item.description:
A fierce nor’wester storm has caused extensive damage in Patnitala upazila of Naogaon district, leaving homes, shops, and infrastructure affected.
The sudden storm disrupted daily life, cut off electricity, and caused significant damage to Boro paddy fields. Minor damage has also been reported in other upazilas of the district.
Late Saturday night, a series of violent thunderstorms and lightning strikes triggered panic among residents. Houses and shops in villages including Chhota Chandpur, Bora Chandpur, Harirampur, Mahmudpur, Chakjoyram, Thuknipara, Notunhat, and Puratan Bazar Palsa were severely affected.
Traffic was disrupted for about two hours after a banyan tree fell at the Sardarpara intersection. Fire service personnel, along with local residents, quickly cleared the debris to restore normal traffic flow.
A massive banyan tree also fell in front of Najipur Model Government High School, blocking the road, while several large trees were uprooted from the Najipur Government College premises.
Shamsul Alam of Chhota Chandpur reported that the tin house he had been building collapsed during the storm. Approximately 400 thatched houses were also destroyed in various areas of Najipur municipality.
Power lines were severed, leading to widespread electricity outages, and telephone and internet services were disrupted.
Local administration and public representatives have visited affected areas and assured residents of necessary assistance. Upazila Nirbahi officer Rifat Ara urged everyone to remain vigilant in the ongoing adverse weather conditions.

item.title: Rajarhat rawhide traders fear losses despite govt price fixing
item.description:
Seasonal rawhide traders in Jessore's Rajarhat are facing growing uncertainty ahead of the upcoming Eid al-Azha.
Rajarhat is a major rawhide market in the southwestern region, drawing traders from ten districts within the Khulna division, as well as prominent merchants from Gopalganj, Faridpur, Rajshahi, Pabna, Ishwardi, and Natore.
Although rawhide trading in the market is seasonal, thousands of traders depend the Eid al-Azha period for their annual income. This year, however, small, medium, and seasonal traders are worried due to rising costs, lack of financing, and uncertainty over fair prices.
The government has fixed the price of salted cowhide at Tk 62–67 per square foot in Dhaka, slightly higher than last year’s Tk 60–65. Outside Dhaka, the rate has been set at Tk 57–62 per square foot, compared to Tk 55–60 last year. Despite the increase, traders doubt whether they will actually receive the government-declared price because of weak market monitoring and irregularities in the leather trade.
Nazrul Islam, a small trader from Rajarhat, said that the cost of preserving hides has risen sharply because salt prices have increased by about Tk 100 per sack. Salt is the main ingredient used to preserve raw hides after sacrifice. He alleged that although the government distributes free salt, small traders rarely receive it. According to him, the salt meant for madrasas and orphanages is often sold elsewhere instead of being used for hide preservation.
Another trader, Anand Das, said small traders cannot access bank loans and are forced to borrow money from NGOs or local moneylenders at high interest rates. Because of this financial pressure, they struggle to make profits and often fail to receive fair prices for the hides they purchase during Qurbani (Eid al Azha)season.
Abdul Malek, a tannery owner in Rajarhat, said tannery owners are owed crores of Taka in unpaid dues. Since these arrears remain unpaid, tannery businesses are suffering from a severe shortage of capital ahead of Eid.
Traders also expressed concern about leather smuggling and weak law enforcement. They believe stricter monitoring is needed to stop illegal trading and ensure fair prices in the market. Some traders argued that Bangladesh’s leather exports are too dependent on China and suggested expanding into European markets to strengthen the industry and protect small and medium traders.
Deputy Commissioner of Jessore Mohammad Ashek Hasan said the district administration has already taken steps to monitor the leather market and prevent smuggling during the Eid.
Rajarhat Rawhide Market operates every Saturday and Tuesday and attracts traders from at least ten districts, including Faridpur, Gopalganj, Madaripur, Jhalokati, Rajshahi, Pabna, Ishwardi, and Natore. The market has around 200 small and large shops and provides employment opportunities for nearly 2,000 people.

Kaarina Kaisar’s body arrives in Dhaka
item.description:
The body of content creator and actor Kaarina Kaisar was brought back to Bangladesh on Sunday.
The flight carrying Karina landed at Hazrat Shahjalal International Airport in Dhaka at 4:50pm. Her father, former national football team captain Kaisar Hamid, received her body at 5:20pm.
Though surrounded by the media, he remained speechless, visibly emotional and teary-eyed.
According to the family, Kaarina’s first namaz-e-janaza will be held after Maghrib prayers at the DOHS field in Banani, Dhaka. A second janaza will take place after Isha prayers at Banani Darbar Sharif. Her body will then be taken to the Central Shaheed Minar at 10pm.
She will be laid to rest after Fajr prayers on Monday in Abdullahpur, Munshiganj.
Kaarina had been in critical condition for several days due to liver-related complications. She was initially placed on life support at a private hospital in the capital. Later, on Monday night, she was flown by air ambulance to Chennai, India, for advanced treatment. She died there on May 15 while undergoing treatment.

Ex-BRUR VC Hasibur Rashid sent to jail in Abu Sayed murder case

Millers for nutrition coalition highlights business potential of fortified wheat flour
item.description:
Millers for Nutrition, powered by TechnoServe, hosted a workshop titled “Technology and Benefits of Introducing Fortified Wheat Flour in the Commercial Market” at Lakeshore Heights, Gulshan, Dhaka, on May 17, 2026, bringing together government, development partners and industry participants.
The workshop aimed to raise awareness of the role of fortified wheat flour in addressing persistent micronutrient deficiencies, particularly Vitamin A, Vitamin B Complex, Iron and Zinc, which remain a pressing public health issue in Bangladesh, especially among women and children.
In the opening remarks, Md Guljer Ahmmed, country program manager at TechnoServe, underscored the need for collaborative action to expand wheat flour fortification in Bangladesh.
The event was attended by several distinguished guests, including chief guest Prof Dr Mohammad Shoeb, member of Bangladesh Food Safety Authority (BFSA), who said: “Fortified wheat flour not only ensures nutrition but also serves as a sustainable public health strategy.” He added, “The time has come for its commercial expansion, as people have become significantly more health-conscious.”
Md S M Abu Sayed, deputy director (CM) of Bangladesh Standards & Testing Institution (BSTI), Md Afif-Al-Mahmud Bhuiyan, deputy director (SDM) of DG Food, and Mir Shakrul Alam Simanto, senior vice president of Flour Mill Owners’ Association of Bangladesh, participated as special guests. Md Zahirul Islam Khan, director (SDM) of DG Food, chaired the event.
The keynote presentation by Md Nayem Zobayer, senior food fortification specialist at TechnoServe, focused on the technological innovations and economic viability of wheat flour fortification. He highlighted it as a scalable, cost-effective and sustainable solution to micronutrient deficiencies.
An open discussion with flour millers, food industry leaders and private sector stakeholders drew support for fortification efforts. Md Moin Uddin Masud, secretary, and Mir Shakrul Alam Simanto expressed optimism that the Government of Bangladesh will soon initiate flour fortification across the OMS program in the country.
Strategic fortification partners Hexagon Nutrition and Buhler, along with World Food Program (WFP), Nutrition International (NI) and Global Alliance for Improved Nutrition (GAIN), also reaffirmed their commitment to supporting food fortification initiatives in Bangladesh. The workshop concluded with remarks from Md Zahirul Islam Khan, who reiterated the collective commitment to scaling up sustainable and inclusive food fortification efforts.

item.title: WHO declares international emergency as Ebola outbreak kills over 80 in DR Congo
item.description:
An Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo has killed more than 80 people as authorities warned there was no vaccine for the strain, in a crisis that the World Health Organization declared an international health emergency on Sunday.
A total of 88 deaths and 336 suspected cases of the highly contagious haemorrhagic fever have been reported, the Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention, or Africa CDC, said in an update on Saturday.
The Geneva-based WHO said early on Sunday the outbreak, caused by the Bundibugyo strain of Ebola, constituted a “public health emergency of international concern,” the second-highest level of alert under international health regulations.
The global health body warned that the true scale of the number of cases and the spread was unclear, but stopped short of declaring a pandemic emergency, the highest alert level introduced in 2024.
Medical aid group Doctors Without Borders, or MSF, said it was preparing a “large-scale response,” calling the rapid spread of the outbreak “extremely concerning,” in warnings echoed by authorities.
“The Bundibugyo strain has no vaccine, no specific treatment,” DR Congo’s Health Minister Samuel-Roger Kamba said.
“This strain has a very high lethality rate, which can reach 50%.”
The strain, which was first identified in 2007, has also killed a Congolese national in neighbouring Uganda, officials said Saturday.
Vaccines are only available for the Zaire strain, which was identified in 1976 and has a higher fatality rate of 60-90%.
Health officials had confirmed the latest outbreak on Friday in Ituri province in northeastern DR Congo, bordering Uganda and South Sudan, according to Africa CDC.
“We’ve been seeing people die for the past two weeks,” said Isaac Nyakulinda, a local civil society representative contacted by AFP by phone.
“There is nowhere to isolate the sick. They are dying at home and their bodies are being handled by their family members.”
According to Kamba, patient zero was a nurse who reported to a health facility in Ituri’s provincial capital Bunia on April 24 with symptoms suggesting Ebola.
Symptoms of the disease include fever, haemorrhaging and vomiting.
“The number of cases and deaths we are seeing in such a short timeframe, combined with the spread across several health zones and now across the border, is extremely concerning,” said Trish Newport, MSF Emergency Programme Manager, which is mobilising medical and support staff to the area.
Large-scale transport of medical equipment is a challenge in DR Congo, a country of more than 100 million people which is four times the size of France but has poor communications infrastructure.
It is the 17th Ebola outbreak to hit DR Congo, and officials warned of a high risk of spread.
“There are significant uncertainties to the true number of infected persons and geographic spread,” the WHO said.
But it added the high positivity rate of initial samples, the confirmation of cases in two countries and the increasing reports of suspected cases “all point towards a potentially much larger outbreak than what is currently being detected and reported, with significant local and regional risk of spread.”
The previous outbreak of Ebola, which has killed around 15,000 people in Africa over the past 50 years despite advances in vaccines and treatment, was last August in the central region.
That episode killed at least 34 people before being declared eradicated in December.
Nearly 2,300 people died in the deadliest outbreak in DR Congo between 2018 and 2020.
Ebola, believed to have originated in bats, can cause severe bleeding and organ failure.
Outbreaks over the past half century have seen a mortality rate among those affected of between 25% and 90%, according to the WHO.
The virus spreads from person to person through bodily fluids or exposure to the blood of an infected person, who becomes contagious only after displaying symptoms. The incubation period can last up to 21 days.

ICT defers July-August 2024 movement case arguments
item.description:
The International Crimes Tribunal-2 on Sunday deferred arguments in a crimes against humanity case over offences committed during the July-August 2024 movement and fixed June 14 for the next hearing for seven accused, including banned Awami League General Secretary Obaidul Quader.
A three-member judicial panel led by Justice Nozrul Islam Chowdhury, chairman of International Crimes Tribunal-2, took the decision after the prosecution sought more time.
Chief Prosecutor Md Aminul Islam appeared for the prosecution and sought time for the arguments. The court then adjourned the hearing and fixed June 14.
Prosecutors Faruk Ahmad, Abdus Sattar Palowan and Mainul Karim were present, along with other prosecutors. State-appointed defence lawyers for the accused were also present.
Earlier, testimony in the case ended on April 27. On that day, investigation officer Ahmed Naser Uddin Mohammad was cross-examined by state-appointed defence lawyers Lokman Howladar and Ishrat Jahan as the 26th witness. His testimony began on April 19.
The other accused in the case are Awami League joint general secretary AFM Bahauddin Nasim, State Minister for Information and Broadcasting of Bangladesh Mohammad Ali Arafat, Jubo League President Sheikh Fazle Shams Parash, Jubo League General Secretary Mainul Hossain Khan Nikhil, Chhatra League President Saddam Hossain and Chhatra League General Secretary Wali Asif Inan.
Formal charges were taken into cognizance on December 18, 2025. On January 22 this year, International Crimes Tribunal-2 ordered the start of the trial by framing charges against the seven accused.

item.title: Cover-up culture starts at home
item.description:
Long before we learned to hide institutional failures, awkward truths, or national embarrassments, we mastered something far more personal: placing a sofa in front of a damp wall and pretending the problem no longer exists.
In Bangladeshi households, concealment has become an interior design philosophy.
Curtains are reassigned to camouflage suspicious stains.
Family portraits perform emergency rescue missions over peeling paint.
Bookshelves stand guard in front of slowly expanding moisture damage like loyal soldiers protecting family reputation.
The wall rots quietly. The room looks fine. Everyone moves on.
Until the smell arrives. Until the paint starts peeling again.
Until allergies, coughing, headaches, and damp air slowly turn the joke into something less funny.
Because damp walls, much like ignored problems everywhere else, do not disappear simply because they are hidden well.
There is a silent revolution happening inside Bangladeshi homes. The sofas have formed a union.
Their manifesto is simple: “Every damp wall deserves a cover-up.”
For decades, they have stood guard -- unwavering, upholstered, and increasingly overworked -- shielding families from the national embarrassment known as “the damp patch.”
Behind them lurks the real enemy: peeling paint, sweating walls, mysterious black stains, and mold colonies thriving like they pay rent.
The sofa did not choose this responsibility. Society assigned it.
And it is not fighting alone.
Curtains have been reassigned from windows to strategic concealment duty.
Wedding portraits now serve as emergency camouflage.
Bookshelves have become frontline defenders of public dignity.
Some furniture has not moved in years because nobody wants to discover what ecosystem may have evolved behind it.
Bangladesh, somehow, has perfected the art of decorative denial.
We spend thousands chasing “glass skin,” hydration, glow, and barrier repair.
Korean serums line dressing tables.
Humidifiers glow softly beside scented candles.
Skincare routines are so elaborate they require tutorial videos.
Meanwhile, the wall beside the bed quietly grows mold powerful enough to undo all of it.
At some point, the moisturizer is no longer fighting dryness -- it is competing with airborne spores.
The irony is almost poetic.
People carefully apply anti-aging cream before sleeping beside walls that look medically concerning.
Air fresheners battle smells that fundamentally belong to “water damage.”
Air purifiers work overtime while mold quietly multiplies behind curtains.
Because damp walls are not merely ugly. They are unhealthy.
Persistent dampness creates the perfect breeding ground for mold, fungus, algae, and bacteria.
These release microscopic particles into the air, affecting indoor air quality and potentially triggering allergies, sinus problems, coughing, skin irritation, headaches, fatigue, and respiratory complications.
For children, elderly family members, and people with asthma or breathing issues, the risks become even more serious.
Many families continue suffering from unexplained allergies or constant discomfort without realizing the wall behind the sofa may actually be contributing to the problem.
And this is not an overreaction. Around the world, dampness and mold are treated as serious health concerns.
In countries like the UK and Germany, moisture control is considered an essential part of healthy housing.
Severe mold exposure has led to legal action, housing violations, and strict building regulations in many parts of Europe and North America.
Meanwhile, in Bangladesh, we simply move the sofa two inches to the left and hope guests do not notice.
The most painful irony, however, is how much money households spend hiding dampness instead of fixing it.
Another coat of paint. Another decorative curtain. Another room freshener.
Another piece of furniture recruited into active service.
Years pass. The wall remains.
At some point, the wall itself deserves treatment.
That is where solutions like Berger Mr. Expert Dampguard One Stop enter the conversation.
Unlike temporary cosmetic fixes, it is designed to address the actual problem rather than helping households continue the national tradition of strategic furniture placement.
The waterproofing solution protects both interior and exterior walls from seepage, persistent dampness, algae growth, and hairline cracks.
Its anti-efflorescence technology helps resist salt and alkali damage, while patented polyester fiber reinforcement makes the protective layer stronger and more durable than conventional sealers or primers.
Flexible coating technology helps cover hairline cracks, while anti-carbonation properties help protect walls from long-term structural damage.
In simple terms, it treats the wall like a real problem -- not a family secret.
Which means the sofa can finally retire from active duty.
And honestly, after decades of emotional labor, it deserves the rest.
